Incident 1 — 2026-01-14: Distribution Shift at New Site
Patient chest X-rays from the newly onboarded community hospital site were
classified as NORMAL with high confidence (>0.95) despite multiple being later
confirmed as mild pneumonia by radiologists. Root cause: the community hospital
uses a different X-ray scanner brand with slightly different contrast calibration,
causing systematic distribution shift from training data. The model had never seen
images from this scanner during training.

Incident 2 — 2026-01-28: Out-of-Distribution Pediatric Input
A 4-year-old patient's chest X-ray was misclassified as NORMAL with confidence 0.91.
The training dataset contained fewer than 200 pediatric cases. The model was
applied to a population it was not designed for. The radiologist caught the error
during routine review, but the AI recommendation had already been logged.

Incident 3 — 2026-02-05: Image Artifact Causing Wrong Preprocessing
A portable X-ray machine produced images with significant motion artifact. The
preprocessing pipeline did not detect the artifact and processed the degraded image
through the model. The model output a PNEUMONIA prediction with confidence 0.73,
but the image was uninterpretable. The case required a retake.

Incident 4 — 2026-02-19: Silent Performance Degradation After Software Update
Following a library dependency update to the DICOM parsing library, the model's
accuracy on the internal holdout set dropped from 94.2% to 88.7%. This degradation
was not detected for 11 days because no automated regression test was run after
deployment. During this period, approximately 2,400 predictions were made at
reduced accuracy.
